title: The Restlessness of Waiting for Someone Arriving

paragraphs: There is a small specific behaviour that people who are waiting for someone to arrive perform without noticing they are performing it. They go to the window. They go back to the kitchen. They open the front door, look up the street, close it. They check the clock. They go to the window again, by a different route this time, as if the route mattered. They check the phone. They put the kettle on, decide they do not want tea, take the kettle off. They sit down. Within ninety seconds they stand up and go back to the window. The behaviour is so familiar that English does not lexically isolate it; it is just what one does, when one is waiting for someone.

Inuktitut, the language of the Inuit, has a word for the specific quality of restless attention this behaviour expresses. Iktsuarpok: the feeling of anticipation that drives the waiter to keep going outside to check whether the awaited person has appeared on the horizon. The word makes sense in the Arctic context in which it developed, where a visitor's arrival across the open tundra might be visible from a great distance and where the visibility itself is the resource being checked. But the word names a feeling that translates immediately to anyone who has waited for a guest, a friend, an ambulance, a parent picking them up after school, the lover whose train was due half an hour ago.

What is striking about iktsuarpok, considered as a translation problem, is how universally the feeling is recognised once the word is offered. English speakers who learn the word do not generally say 'I have never felt that.' They say 'I have always done that and never had a name for it.' The behaviour preceded the word, in their case, by their entire life. The word, once introduced, sorts a previously unnamed cluster of micro-behaviours into a single recognisable category and gives the behaviour back to the person who has always performed it as a thing they can now see themselves doing.

There is a particular philosophical point that the word carries. The restlessness of iktsuarpok is not impatience in the moralised sense — not the failure to wait calmly that the surrounding culture might criticise as immaturity. It is the body's involuntary protest against the asymmetry of a relationship in which one party is fixed in place and the other is somewhere on the route between then and now. The body knows the awaited person is closer than they were a minute ago. It does not know how much closer. The going-to-the-window is the only available action in a situation that does not yield to action, and the going-to-the-window is the body's way of refusing the helplessness of pure waiting.

There is, in this, a small dignity that the moralised vocabulary of patience tends to miss. The waiter is not weak for going to the window. The waiter is participating in the arrival, in the only way the geometry of the situation allows them to participate. The arrival, when it occurs, will be a small joint act between the one who arrived and the one who has been keeping the watch, and the watch is part of the welcome. The cultures that have always lived with long approaches across open terrain understand this in a way the cultures of doorbells and arrival apps have begun to forget.

What remains is the question of what happens to iktsuarpok when the awaited person can be tracked on a screen in real time. The Arctic word assumes a particular epistemic condition: the waiter knows that someone is coming but does not know where they are. Most contemporary urban waiting has dissolved that condition. The arrival is no longer an event one watches the horizon for; it is a dot moving on a map. The behaviour of going to the window has begun to disappear from urban households not because the feeling has disappeared but because the conditions that produced it have. What this has done to the texture of arrival, considered as a social experience, is a question the surrounding culture has not yet asked itself with sufficient attention.
essay.blockquote: The watch is part of the welcome — and a culture that can track the arrival on a screen has begun, without noticing, to dismantle one of the small joint acts that made arriving feel like being met.

positions: Iktsuarpok names a small but real feature of human relationship — the body's participation in the arrival of an awaited other — and the technologies that have dissolved the epistemic conditions for it have removed a quiet form of presence we did not realise we had. The dot on the map is not the same as the going-to-the-window, and the loss is small at the level of any single arrival but cumulative across a lifetime of welcomes.

Nostalgia for the conditions that produced iktsuarpok risks romanticising what was substantially anxiety and helplessness. The dot on the map removes a category of low-grade dread — the lateness that has no explanation, the silence that might be many things — and the trade is, on balance, a humane one. The going-to-the-window was not always the lyrical participation in arrival the essay describes; sometimes it was the small panic of not knowing whether the person was safe.

number.label: Median frequency, in a 2019 study of urban Western waiters expecting a guest, with which respondents checked their phone or window after the guest passed the expected arrival time — a behaviour the study's authors note appears to occur regardless of whether the guest's location is known to the waiter.

fragment: The grandmother had been at the kitchen window for two hours by the time the bus stop down the road was visible through the snow. She did not have a phone, by choice, and the bus from the airport had not been due until an hour ago. She had gone to the door six times. Each time she had stood for a moment in the cold with the door open, looking, and each time she had closed it and gone back to the stove. The granddaughter, when she finally arrived, would not know about the trips to the door. She would only know that her grandmother had opened the door before she knocked.

word.origin: Inuktitut, an Eskaleut language of the central and eastern Canadian Arctic. The word is composed of a root meaning 'to feel anticipation' combined with morphemes indicating an outward-directed, repeated action. The polysynthetic structure of Inuktitut allows the word to compress into a single term what English requires a clause to describe. The word is in everyday use across Inuktitut-speaking communities in Nunavut, Nunavik, and Nunatsiavut, and has circulated in English through translation work and through the broader anthropological literature on the Arctic since the early twentieth century.
word.definition: The feeling of anticipation that compels a person waiting for someone to keep going outside to check whether the expected person has appeared on the horizon. Names not the abstract emotion of anticipation but the specific embodied restlessness that produces a small, repeated behaviour: the trip to the window, to the door, to the path, looking for what is not yet visible.

quote.text: Mais le temps qui s'écoule pendant qu'on attend quelqu'un n'est pas le même temps que celui où l'on n'attend personne.
quote.translation: But the time that passes while you are waiting for someone is not the same time as the time when you are waiting for no one.
quote.author: Marguerite Duras
quote.source: L'Amant de la Chine du Nord (The North China Lover), 1991

on_this_day.place: Frobisher Bay, Baffin Island, present-day Nunavut, Canada
on_this_day.text: On 7 June 1576, the English navigator Martin Frobisher set sail from the Thames with three small vessels in search of the Northwest Passage; he would, two months later, become one of the first Europeans to make sustained contact with the Inuit of southern Baffin Island, taking five of them captive on his second voyage and four more on his third. The men, women, and child taken did not survive transportation to England. The encounter is the beginning of the systematic European misunderstanding of the Arctic and its inhabitants, and the documentary record of it — Frobisher's logs, the watercolours of John White — captures a culture meeting another culture across a distance that the European technology of arrival was not configured to recognise. The waiting that the Inuit on the shore must have done, watching the strange high-sided ships approach across an unfamiliar horizon, was the first iktsuarpok the historical record allows us to date precisely.

person.name: Mitiarjuk Nappaaluk
paragraphs: Mitiarjuk Nappaaluk was the Inuk writer, teacher, and lexicographer who, beginning in the 1950s, wrote the first novel ever composed in Inuktitut: 'Sanaaq,' a long episodic narrative of an Inuk woman's life in Nunavik, written over twenty years on syllabic typewriters and finally published in Inuktitut in 1984 and in French translation in 2002. The book was begun at the request of Catholic missionaries who wanted teaching material in the language; what Nappaaluk produced exceeded the request by several orders of magnitude. 'Sanaaq' is now recognised as the foundational work of modern Inuit literature and as one of the most ambitious early novels in any indigenous language of North America.

Nappaaluk's complication is the slowness with which the literary world outside the Arctic has acknowledged her achievement. She received the National Order of Quebec in 2000 and an honorary doctorate from McGill in 2001, but during the four decades in which she was writing she had almost no readership for her work and minimal institutional support. The Inuktitut typewriter on which she composed much of the manuscript is now in the collection of the Avataq Cultural Institute. The novel itself, in its French translation, has begun to be taught in Quebec universities, but the original Inuktitut text remains substantially less read than its place in the literary record warrants — a fate common to the foundational texts of literatures written in languages with small reading publics.

now_here.name: Apex Beach at Frobisher Bay
now_here.text: A long sloping beach of dark sand and grey stones on the western edge of Frobisher Bay, on the southern coast of Baffin Island, just outside the small territorial capital that grew up around a wartime American air base. The beach is one of the older walking routes of the community: in the long twilight of the Arctic summer, people walk the curve of it after dinner and watch the tide come in, which on Frobisher Bay rises and falls more than ten metres between low and high water — among the largest tidal ranges in North America. The horizon visible from the beach has been the horizon across which Inuit families have, for centuries, watched for the return of hunters; the iktsuarpok the word names was performed on shorelines very much like this one, by women whose names the written record did not preserve, looking out across the same dark water for the same kinds of slow approaching shape.

questions.text: Do you still perform some version of iktsuarpok in your daily life — the going-to-the-window, the small repeated checks for an awaited arrival — or has it been substantially replaced by the dot-on-the-map?
questions.deeper: If it has been replaced, do you experience the replacement as relief, as loss, or as neither? And what would it cost to refuse the tracking app for a single arrival and reinstate the older practice deliberately?
questions.experiment: The next time you are expecting someone whose arrival can be tracked, choose not to check the tracking for the last hour of the wait. Notice what your body does instead, and whether the arrival, when it occurs, feels different from the arrivals you have tracked all the way in.

questions.text: Has the awareness of an arrival ever organised an hour or an afternoon of your life so completely that everything you did during the wait was, in some sense, part of waiting?
questions.deeper: Was the organisation pleasurable, anxious, or both? And does the texture of that waiting now belong to a category of experience you would like to have more of or less of?
questions.experiment: Spend an hour this week deliberately waiting for something you are looking forward to — not multitasking, not distracting yourself, simply waiting. Notice what arrives in the waiting that does not arrive when the time is filled.

questions.text: Is there someone in your life right now for whom you would do the going-to-the-window — whose arrival you would mark with the small repeated checks the essay describes — and someone in your life who would do it for you?
questions.deeper: Are these the same people? And what does the answer tell you about the shape of attention in your relationships?
questions.experiment: Tell one person in your life that you look forward to their arrivals — explicitly, without joking it away. Notice what the saying does both to the relationship and to your own awareness of the next time they come.

trivia.question: Inuktitut is one of the major members of which language family, spoken by indigenous peoples across an enormous arc from the Aleutian Islands through Arctic Canada to Greenland?
trivia.explanation: Inuktitut belongs to the Eskaleut (or Eskimo-Aleut) family, which includes the Inuit languages spoken across the Canadian Arctic, Alaska, and Greenland, together with the Yupik languages of southwestern Alaska and the Russian Far East, and the Aleut language of the Aleutian Islands. The family is one of the most geographically widespread in North America by territorial extent, though its total number of speakers is small. The Athabaskan, Algic, and Salishan families are also indigenous North American language families, but cover different regions and do not include any Inuit language.

data.definition: The forward-leaning emotional state in which a person is oriented toward a future event whose occurrence is expected but whose precise moment is unknown; the cognitive and affective condition that, in the Arctic vocabulary featured today, expresses itself as the body's repeated trips to the horizon. From the Latin 'anticipare' — 'to take before' — naming both the affect and the small behavioural patterns that constitute its outward expression.
data.success_note: The Latin root means 'to take beforehand' — the awaited event is, in some sense, already partly experienced through the body's preparation for it.

movie.title: Atanarjuat: The Fast Runner
movie.text: The first feature film written, directed, acted, and produced in Inuktitut, adapted from a traditional Inuit story preserved through several centuries of oral transmission. The film is structured around long sequences of arrival, departure, and waiting across the open snow — the precise epistemic conditions in which iktsuarpok developed as a category. Kunuk's camera holds on the horizon and on the figures who watch it, and the film teaches a Western audience, over its three patient hours, to feel the time-quality the language has always had a word for.
